candidate_problems.text: What are the operational, measurable indicators that the 'control structure' linking capability growth to loss of human steering power has actually been broken, rather than merely displaced or hidden? The conjecture depends on a high-level structural invariant, but it leaves open how to define, observe, and falsify that invariant across technical, organizational, and geopolitical levels.

Is there a principled reason to expect capability growth to couple to opacity, strategic behavior, and reduced corrigibility, or is that coupling contingent on current architectures and deployment incentives? This is a core unresolved tension because the conjecture treats the coupling as the main danger signal, yet it remains unclear whether it is a deep law-like tendency or an artifact of present design choices and institutions.

Can scalable governance and alignment mechanisms remain effective under competitive pressure when actors can gain by defecting, decentralizing, or hiding capability? The conjecture says institutional control must scale with technical power, but the open question is whether such control is dynamically stable in the real world or whether selection pressures will systematically erode it faster than safety methods improve.
